item.title: Metal Gear Solid 2's HD Port Just Had Its Entire Source Code Leaked
item.description: The HD version, specifically its Vita port, has been busted wide open all these years later.
item.author: (Quinton O'Connor)

item.title: Agentic Coding is a Trap | Lars Faye
item.description: Remaining vigilant about cognitive debt and atrophy.

item.title: Spirit 2.0 — The Airline Owned by the People, for the People
item.description: Spirit Airlines collapsed. Before private equity locks it up, the people can own it. Join the Spirit 2.0 founding coalition. One member, one vote. Profits shared by all.

item.title: GitHub - aattaran/deepclaude: Use Claude Code's autonomous agent loop with DeepSeek V4 Pro, OpenRouter, or any Anthropic-compatible backend. Same UX, 17x cheaper.
item.description: Use Claude Code's autonomous agent loop with DeepSeek V4 Pro, OpenRouter, or any Anthropic-compatible backend. Same UX, 17x cheaper. - aattaran/deepclaude

item.title: A desktop made for one
item.description: Philosophy - Sciences - Geekery - Art - Life - Coaching - Fun < Simplify Everything

item.title: nullagent (@nullagent@partyon.xyz)
item.description:
Announcing the BYOMesh!
We've been hard at work designing what I believe is now the worlds SMALLEST & most CAPABLE LoRa companion dev kit ever!
It combines the tried and true SX1276 allowing access to the full sub-1GHz ISM band all mesh enthusiast know and love, along with the SX1281 providing high speed 2.4GHz LoRa all on ONE tiny board.
https://shop.dataparty.xyz/products/byomesh
@dataparty @rfparty
#BYOMesh #LoRa #MeshCore #Meshtastic #MeshTNC #Reticulum #BLE #SnoopUntoThemAsTheySnoopUntoUs

item.title: Why TUIs are back by Alcides Fonseca
item.description: Terminal User Interfaces (TUIs) are making a comeback. DHH’s Omarchy is made of three types of user interfaces: TUIs, for immediate feedback and bonus geek points, webapps because 37signals (his company) sells SAAS web applications and the unavoidable gnome-style native applications that really do not fit well in the style of the distro.

item.title: Feedback Loops :: Faster Safely
item.description: Feedback loops are crucial when trying to modify a system or build something new. Engineers can only safely move as fast as their comprehension of how a change impacts a system. The abilities and limitations of how computers and humans process information is massively important here; including how feedback information is stored, retrieved, and presented to systems and engineers.

item.title: 5 Years of Freelancing: same work, better pay, and a ceiling – Dylan Castillo
When is Nextcloud Overkill? | Samuele Amato
item.description: When is Nextcloud Overkill?

item.title: Boolean logic operators are not intuitive. Let's do better
item.description:
Quick! What’s the truth table of XNOR?

If you don’t catch that, don’t worry. Hopefully by
item.author: (map[email:charon@konekopi.com name:Evans Jahja])

item.title: Why you should still type code in 2026
item.description: Why you should still type code in 2026

item.title: March 2026 Top 40 New CRAN Packages – R Works
item.description: An idiosyncratic, unabashedly biased, time-constrained attempt to capture the depth and breadth of the new packages submitted to CRAN in a single month entirely without the use of AI: clearly unsustainable, but maybe entertaining and somewhat useful.

item.title: Babysitting the Agent · Two weeks in, even with all the hooks I've built, working with the agent has become a chore. Every shipped feature ends with me clicking through it to find out what didn't actually work.
item.description: Two weeks in, even with all the hooks I've built, working with the agent has become a chore. Every shipped feature ends with me clicking through it to find out what didn't actually work.
item.author: (Christopher Meiklejohn)

item.title: Do LLMs Reason, or Do They Just Predict Math Text?
item.description: When “predicts the next number” gets published as “knows how to reason.” INGA314.ai analysis of KisMATH (Saha et al., TACL 2026) TL;DR. KisMATH is a real and useful dat…

item.title: How I freed a pool heat pump from an unencrypted Chinese cloud server
item.description: I have a pre-installed pool heat pump - an “AcquaSource” branded unit, the kind you can buy at any pool store in Europe - which supports WiFi. The App called “Pool Panel” wasn’t pretty, but it worked and I didn’t give it much thought. At one time, the remote control of the pump stopped responding: The pump itself was fine; the panel worked, the temperature held. So I decided to take a deeper look at how it all works. It turned out to be a security nightmare.

item.title: Hit and Run and a Business Idea
item.description: Rental car insurance is broken in Dubai. After being the victim of a hit and run, I believe there's a business opportunity to make it better.
item.author: (rozumem)

item.title: vLLM Real-World Lab Report
item.description: The vLLM real-world lab models mixed production traffic instead of a single throughput number. FCK generated the request mixes, ran the scheduler and routing profiles, captured build outcomes, and emitted the evidence used for the charts below. The lab covers six request classes: interactive chat, RAG with repeated prefixes, long-prefill requests, agent tool loops, batch summarization, and slow streaming clients.

item.title: erm: A Local CLI That Strips Ums, Uhs, and Erms From Speech
item.description: A tour of erm, a local CLI that removes disfluencies from English speech recordings using faster-whisper, a few detectors that look at the audio directly, and ffmpeg.
item.author: (Doug Calobrisi)

item.title: Using IDEs as a Harness for Thinking and Writing
item.description: Why your primary AI workflow should be notes and files in an IDE, not a chat window. The IDE has become the harness for serious human thinking and writing.

item.title: No smoking gun
item.description: The personal blog of Tim White, Software Engineer from Somerset, UK.

item.title: Your Container Is Not a Sandbox
item.description: The microVM ecosystem was battle-tested long before agentic AI created the demand. A landscape survey: every VMM, the shared Rust crate ecosystem, a dozen AI sandbox platforms, and honest trade-offs.
item.author: (Emir Beganović)

item.title: CPU work and GPU work · Tal Hoffman
item.description: The discourse around AI agents has gotten ahead of the practice. Most of what you read assumes we already know which work to point them at, and the rest treats the question as one of how aggressive to be rather than whether the task even fits. What's missing is not urgency but clarity about which work actually fits.

item.title: Lzon.ca. A personal blog, by a programmer and IT expert.
item.description: A personal blog, by a programmer and IT expert. Essays, Articles, Guides, and Recipes. As well as Code, Quotes, and Links.

item.title: My Agent Memory Library Helps Write Indie Articles
item.description: Can a custom memory library help an agent write good indie dev articles? We test it live: elfmem-grounded research, vault recall, peer agent communication, and a human editor. Here is what came out.
item.author: (Ben Emson)

item.title: How I Use Codex CLI to Write and Maintain a Book on Codex CLI
item.description: I have a 32-chapter book about Codex CLI that updates itself daily. An always-on agent named Andy (the default name in NanoClaw, because I…
item.author: (https://blog.danielvaughan.com)

item.title: Writing a bindless GPU abstraction layer
item.description: Back in December 2025, Sebastian Aaltonen published a blog titled “No Graphics API” - it presented a great history of the evolution of GPU hardware, and gave an opinionated perspective on how we could simplify the modern graphics APIs on modern hardware. Like many graphics programmers, I read Sebastian’s blog post and really enjoyed it - I was inspired by it, and decided to see how close to the API he describes I could get today, layered on top of existing platform APIs. The answer turns out to be “pretty darn close”. The result is a project I’m calling Loon GPU, and I’ve put it up on Github. While the library is still rough, poorly tested and surely filled with bugs, it is usable and I wanted to share it early in case other folks were interested. Currently it has a Vulkan 1.3 and Metal 4 backend, and here I want to do a high-level walkthrough to see how the API design maps on to those backends.

item.title: Prompt Engineering Is Permanent
item.description: I thought better models would make prompt engineering disappear. Instead, they became even more important.

item.title: acai.sh
item.description: The toolkit for spec-driven development. Write feature specs, not prompts. Ship better software with AI agents that understand your requirements.

item.title: A Couple Million Lines of Haskell: Production Engineering at Mercury | The Haskell Programming Language's blog
item.description: What it takes to run 2 million lines of Haskell in production at a fintech company serving 300,000 businesses.

item.title: An open-weights Chinese model just beat Claude, GPT-5.5, and Gemini in a programming challenge - ThinkPol
item.description: By Rohana Rezel I’m running the ongoing AI Coding Contest where I pit major language models against each other in real-time programming tasks with objective scoring. Day 12 was the Word Gem Puzzle. Ten models entered. The results were not what most people would have predicted. Kimi K2.6, an open-weights model from Chinese startup Moonshot

item.title: Why I don't like the "staff engineer archetypes"
item.description: The most influential piece of writing about staff engineers in the last decade has to be Will Larson’s Staff engineer archetypes. He argues that the “staff engineer” title covers at least four very different roles: the team lead, the architect, the solver, and the right hand. This taxonomy gets cited a lot as advice for people who are trying to become effective staff engineers. For both of my promotions to staff engineer, my manager at the time linked me to the “staff engineer archetypes” and asked me to consider which of these archetypes I was aiming towards.
